card.intuition: Optimizing how software reads your website aloud to blind or visually impaired users. It involves creating a clean 'Accessibility Tree' that mirrors your visual layout.
card.analogy: A radio play of a movie: if the movie only has cool graphics and no sound, the radio listener (Screen Reader user) has no idea what's happening. You need to add 'narrative descriptions' (Alt text, ARIA, Labels) so the story makes sense without the pictures.
when_to_use: Testing your site with tools like NVDA, JAWS, or VoiceOver
Adding 'alt' text to complex images and diagrams
Ensuring form inputs have associated `<label>` tags
gotchas: A common mistake is using `title` attributes for everything; screen readers often ignore them. Use `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` instead
If an image is purely 'decorative' (like a background flourish), give it an empty alt tag (`alt=""`) so the screen reader skips it instead of reading the filename

steps.label: DOM
steps.annotation: Browser builds DOM and sends to accessibility tree.

steps.label: Accessibility tree
steps.annotation: Accessibility Object Model derived: roles, names, states, properties.

steps.label: ARIA
steps.annotation: ARIA attributes patch the accessibility tree where HTML semantics fall short.

steps.label: Screen reader
steps.annotation: Screen reader reads accessibility tree — announces role, name, state.

steps.label: Keyboard
steps.annotation: User navigates with keyboard — focus management must be explicit for SPAs.

exercise.prompt: Identify the HTML element used to specify the text description for an image.
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.lower() == "alt"
assert check("alt")
print("PASS")
